questions.question: In the Angular component model, what role does the **class** decorated with `@Component` play?
options: It is the controller: its fields hold state and its methods hold behavior that the template binds to
It is a configuration-only object; all state and behavior must live in a separate controller class
It defines the CSS scoping strategy and nothing else
It is the template, written in TypeScript instead of HTML
questions.explanation: The reviewer states the class is the controller — fields are state, methods are behavior — and that no separate 'controller' concept exists. The decorator supplies metadata; the class holds state/behavior.

questions.question: Since v19, components are standalone by default, so you set `standalone: false` only when you want to keep the old NgModule-based behavior.
questions.explanation: The reviewer says components are standalone by default since v19 (the `standalone: true` flag is implied), and `standalone: false` is set only to keep the old NgModule behavior.

questions.question: What does the Angular style guide recommend regarding a component's `selector` prefix?
options: Always use `app-` because it is reserved by the framework
Choose your own short project-specific prefix (e.g. `yt-`, `mr-`) to avoid collisions; `app-` is merely the CLI default
Omit any prefix so selectors stay short
Use the `ng-` prefix to match Angular's built-in elements
questions.explanation: The reviewer says `app-` is the CLI default, but the style guide recommends picking your own project-specific prefix to avoid collisions with native and third-party tags. `app-` is not reserved.

questions.question: In Angular's binding grammar, which syntax flows an **event out** of the DOM to a handler on the class?
options: `[prop]="expr"`
`{{ expr }}`
`(event)="handler($event)"`
questions.explanation: Parentheses `(event)` send events up to the class. Square brackets push data down, interpolation renders text, and the banana-in-a-box `[()]` combines both directions.

questions.question: When does a **pure** pipe (the default) re-run its `transform()`?
options: On every change-detection cycle
Only when its input *reference* (or an argument) changes
Only when any signal anywhere in the component changes
Never after the first render; the result is cached permanently
questions.explanation: A pure pipe recomputes only when the input reference changes (a new primitive, object, array, or Date reference). Running every CD cycle describes an impure pipe.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to `[attr.colspan]="2"` rather than `[colspan]="2"` for a table cell?
options: `[attr.x]` is faster because Angular caches attribute writes
Property binding only works on form controls, never on table cells
`colspan` has no corresponding DOM property, so it must be set as an attribute via attribute binding
Property binding would set a string, while attribute binding preserves the number type
questions.explanation: Attribute binding (`[attr.x]`) exists for attributes that have no matching DOM property — `colspan`, `aria-*`, SVG attributes. The type-preservation claim is backwards: property binding (not attribute binding) preserves type.

questions.question: A template binds `[disabled]="isBusy"` where `isBusy = signal(false)`. What is the bug and its effect?
options: The signal must be wrapped in `()` to read its value; binding `isBusy` without parentheses binds the signal function object, which is always truthy, so the button is permanently disabled
Signals cannot be used in property bindings at all; only interpolation supports them
`[disabled]` expects a string, so the boolean signal is coerced to the literal text `false` and the button stays enabled
Nothing is wrong; Angular auto-invokes signals in bindings
questions.explanation: Reading a signal requires calling it: `isBusy()`. Without parentheses you bind the function object, which is always truthy, so `[disabled]` is permanently true. The call is also what registers the template as a dependency.

questions.question: Into what does the two-way binding `[(value)]="expr"` desugar?
options: `[value]="expr"` plus `(value)="expr = $event"`
`[value]="expr"` plus `(valueChange)="expr = $event"`
`(value)="expr"` plus `(valueChange)="expr"`
A single `[value]` binding with internal mutation tracking
questions.explanation: Two-way binding desugars to a property binding `[value]` plus an event binding `(valueChange)`, following the `x` / `xChange` naming convention (also satisfied by `model()` signals).

questions.question: Using `[(ngModel)]="query"` on an input requires importing `FormsModule` into the component.
questions.explanation: The reviewer explicitly notes that `[(ngModel)]` two-way binding for form controls requires importing `FormsModule`.

questions.question: What does `ViewEncapsulation.None` do to a component's styles?
options: Attaches a native shadow root so styles are truly isolated from the page
Rewrites selectors with generated per-component attributes to scope them
Injects the styles globally with no rewriting, so they affect the whole document
Blocks all global styles from reaching the component's elements
questions.explanation: `None` turns off scoping: styles are injected globally and affect the whole page (a footgun outside theme/reset components). Attribute rewriting is `Emulated`; a real shadow root is `ShadowDom`.

questions.question: What does the `:host-context(.dark) .card { ... }` selector target?
options: The component's own host element only when it has a `.dark` class
`.card` elements inside the component, but only when some *ancestor* of the host matches `.dark`
Any `.card` element anywhere in the document under a `.dark` ancestor
Child components nested inside the host that carry a `.dark` class
questions.explanation: `:host-context(selector)` styles content based on an ancestor of the host matching the selector — e.g. a `.dark` theme class up the tree. `:host(.active)` (not host-context) styles the host itself by its own class.

questions.question: Which statements about `::ng-deep` are correct per the reviewer? (Select all that apply.)
options: It forces a style past the encapsulation boundary into descendant components
The Angular team strongly discourages new use; it remains only for backwards compatibility
It is the recommended modern replacement for component inputs and CSS custom properties
There is currently no standards-track replacement for it
It is required whenever you use `ViewEncapsulation.Emulated`
questions.explanation: `::ng-deep` pierces the encapsulation boundary into descendants, is discouraged and kept only for backwards compatibility, and has no standards-track replacement. It is the thing being replaced (by inputs/custom properties), not a replacement, and is never required.

questions.question: Which statements about Angular pipes are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Pipes are pure-presentation: they format a value without mutating the source
Pipes chain left-to-right, the output of one feeding the next
Custom pipes are written with `@Pipe` and a `transform()` method, implementing `PipeTransform`
Since v19 you must import `CommonModule` to use any built-in pipe
An impure pipe is the default and runs only on input-reference change
questions.explanation: Pipes are non-mutating display formatters, chain left-to-right, and custom ones use `@Pipe`/`transform()`/`PipeTransform`. Pipes are standalone by default (no `CommonModule` ceremony), and pure (re-run on reference change) is the default — impure is opt-in via `pure: false`.

questions.question: Given the handler below bound via `(input)="onType($event)"`, what is `$event` and why is the cast needed?
options: `$event` is the input's string value; the cast converts it to an element
`$event` is the native DOM `Event`; `e.target` is typed as a generic `EventTarget`, so it is narrowed to `HTMLInputElement` to read `.value`
`$event` is the component instance; the cast accesses its `value` field
`$event` is the emitted output value; no cast is actually required
questions.explanation: For a DOM input event, `$event` is the native `Event`. Its `target` is the broad `EventTarget` type, so it must be narrowed (cast) to `HTMLInputElement` before reading `.value`. For component outputs, by contrast, `$event` is the emitted value.

questions.question: When deriving a displayed value, when does the reviewer favor a **pipe** over a `computed` signal?
options: Always — pipes memoize better than computed signals
For display formatting reused across multiple templates; `computed` is preferred for derived application state in the class
Whenever the value depends on signals, since computed cannot read signals
Only for impure transformations, since computed cannot memoize
questions.explanation: The reviewer recommends a pipe for display formatting reused across templates, and `computed` for derived application state in the class (it memoizes on its signal dependencies). Computed can read signals and does memoize, so the other options are wrong.

questions.question: Why are `SlicePipe` and `KeyValuePipe` declared impure (`pure: false`)?
options: Because they perform asynchronous work and must re-subscribe each cycle
Because their output depends on in-place mutation of the array/object, which a pure pipe would not detect
Because impure pipes are faster than pure pipes for collections
Because they must run before change detection to validate their input
questions.explanation: A pure pipe only re-runs on a reference change and would miss in-place mutation of the collection. `Slice` and `KeyValue` operate over arrays/objects that may be mutated in place, so they are impure to stay correct (at a performance cost). `AsyncPipe` is impure for subscription reasons, but Slice/KeyValue are about mutation.

questions.question: A component is configured as below. Per the reviewer, how do **global/external** stylesheet rules interact with this component's elements?
options: Global styles are blocked from entering; only `ExperimentalIsolatedShadowDom` allows them in
Global styles still pierce into the component; `ExperimentalIsolatedShadowDom` is the variant that blocks them from entering
Global styles leak both in and out freely, identical to `None`
Global styles are blocked in both directions, since any shadow root is fully isolated
questions.explanation: With `ShadowDom`, the component's styles are isolated outward but global styles still pierce in. The experimental `ExperimentalIsolatedShadowDom` (v21) additionally blocks external/global styles from entering the ShadowRoot.

questions.question: Per the v22 baseline, which describes the default change-detection behavior and the former `Default` strategy?
options: `OnPush` is the default and checks the component on input-reference changes, events, or signal updates; the former `Default` is deprecated and renamed `Eager`, which checks the whole subtree every pass
`Default` remains the default and checks only on input changes; `OnPush` must be opted into for event-driven checks
`Eager` is the default and checks on signal updates only; `OnPush` checks the whole subtree every pass
`OnPush` is the default and checks the whole subtree on every pass, identical to the old `Default`
questions.explanation: Since v22, `OnPush` is the default — it checks on input-reference changes, events, or signal updates. The old always-check `Default` strategy is deprecated and renamed `Eager`, which checks the whole subtree every pass; you set `Eager` explicitly only to restore that behavior.
